rationale: The citing paper uses Zhao, Hoeffler, and Zauberman (2007) as a direct prior benchmark in the preferences-over-time stream, explicitly noting their process- versus outcome-simulation account for reducing temporal preference inconsistency. It then positions its own contribution as adding a different mechanism - social and temporal distance fit in peer recommendations - to shift preferences over time. The cited study is also used methodologically, with Study 2 stimuli and design logic adapted from that prior work to establish expected near-versus-distant baseline preferences.
relation_description: The 2011 paper extends Zhao et al. (2007) by proposing and testing an alternative route to temporal preference shift: the construal-level congruency of recommendation source and decision time. It builds on the same intertemporal preference-consistency problem and reuses/adapts that prior paradigm while introducing a new social-distance mechanism.
